news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan las estrategias de adaptación de la naturaleza, la importancia de sensibilizar sobre cuestiones clave, el cambio hacia sistemas de calefacción doméstica eficientes desde el punto de vista energético y la necesidad de integrar la economía medioambiental más allá de las medidas tradicionales.

articles.title: Oak trees are delaying spring to starve caterpillars
articles.summary: Oak trees have a surprising trick to fight back against hungry caterpillars: they simply wait. When trees are heavily attacked one year, they delay leaf growth by just three days the next spring—long enough to leave newly hatched caterpillars with nothing to eat. This small shift slashes insect survival and reduces leaf damage by more than half, proving even more efficient than costly chemical defenses.
articles.content:
In spring forests, timing is everything. Many insects, especially caterpillars, hatch just as tree leaves are young, tender, and full of nutrients. This perfect alignment gives them immediate access to food, allowing them to begin feeding right away.

But oak trees have a surprising response when caterpillars become too abundant. If a tree experiences heavy infestation in one year, it shifts its schedule the next spring by delaying leaf emergence by about three days. For caterpillars, this small delay has big consequences. When they hatch, the leaves they depend on are still sealed inside buds, leaving them with nothing to eat.

This simple adjustment proves remarkably effective. A delay of just a few days sharply lowers caterpillar survival and cuts feeding damage to the trees by about 55 percent. These findings were reported by an international research team in the journal Nature Ecology & Evolution.

Trees Actively Respond to Insect Threats

"The delaying tactic is more effective for the oak than a chemical defense, such as bitter tannins in the leaves," says Dr. Soumen Mallick, a postdoc at the University of Würzburg's Biocentre and lead author of the study. Producing more tannins would require a large energy investment from the tree, making timing a more efficient defense.

"This discovery fundamentally changes our previous understanding of the onset of spring in the forest," says the Würzburg researcher. Instead of simply responding to temperature and weather, trees can also adjust their timing based on biological threats like insect outbreaks.

Satellite Data Reveals Forest-Wide Patterns

To uncover these patterns, the researchers combined ecological studies with advanced remote sensing technology. In the past, scientists relied on detailed ground observations of individual trees. In this case, the team monitored a much larger area, covering 2,400 square kilometers in Northern Bavaria, using Sentinel-1 radar satellites.

These satellites are particularly useful because they can capture accurate data on tree canopies even through dense cloud cover. Over five years, from 2017 to 2021, the researchers collected 137,500 observations. The data had a resolution of 10x10 meters per pixel, roughly matching the size of a single tree crown. In total, 27,500 pixels across 60 forest sites were analyzed.

The year 2019 offered a key opportunity to observe this process in action, as a major gypsy moth outbreak hit the region. "The radar sensors recorded exactly which trees were stripped bare and how they reacted in the following year," says Professor Jörg Müller, University of Würzburg (Germany) Chair of Conservation Biology and Forest Ecology and co-senior author of the study.

Forest Timing Reflects an Evolutionary Tug of War

The results help explain why forests do not always turn green as early as rising temperatures might suggest. This insight is important for conservation efforts, since many existing models focus mainly on environmental factors like temperature while overlooking interactions between plants and insects.

Trees are caught in a balancing act. Warmer conditions linked to climate change encourage earlier leaf growth, while pressure from insects pushes trees to delay it. One advantage of this strategy is its flexibility. Trees only delay leafing after actual infestations, which prevents insects from adapting to the change over time.

"This dynamic interplay is an example of the forest's high resilience and adaptability in a changing world," says Professor Andreas Prinzing, University of Rennes (France), the other co-senior author of the study. Future research aims to explore these mechanisms in even greater detail.

Participating Research Institutions

In addition to researchers from the University of Würzburg, scientists from the following institutions were involved in this study:
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant ecological discovery showing how oak trees adaptively delay leaf growth to reduce caterpillar damage, demonstrating a natural, energy-efficient defense mechanism with broad implications for forest resilience and conservation under climate change.
articles.personality_title: Oak trees delay spring leaves to starve hungry caterpillars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In spring, many caterpillars hatch just as oak trees grow new leaves. These young leaves are soft and full of nutrients, making them perfect food for caterpillars.

**What happened** – Scientists found that when oak trees are heavily eaten by caterpillars one year, they delay growing new leaves by about three days the next spring. This small delay means caterpillars hatch before leaves open, leaving them with no food. Researchers used satellite data from Northern Bavaria between 2017 and 2021 to see this pattern over large forests.

**Impact** – This delay lowers caterpillar survival and cuts leaf damage by more than half. It works better than the tree’s chemical defenses, like bitter tannins, and uses less energy. The discovery shows trees don’t just follow temperature changes but also react to insect threats, changing how we understand forest spring timing.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to study this defense in more detail and see how it helps forests adapt to climate change. This could improve forest protection and how we predict forest growth in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Oak trees can delay leaf growth after heavy caterpillar attacks, using a natural timing trick to protect themselves and reduce damage.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les chênes retardent le printemps pour affamer les chenilles affamées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au printemps, de nombreuses chenilles éclosent juste au moment où les chênes développent de nouvelles feuilles. Ces feuilles jeunes sont tendres et riches en nutriments, parfaites pour nourrir les chenilles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert que lorsque les chênes sont fortement attaqués par les chenilles une année, ils retardent la croissance de leurs nouvelles feuilles d’environ trois jours au printemps suivant. Ce petit retard fait éclore les chenilles avant l’ouverture des feuilles, ce qui les prive de nourriture. Les chercheurs ont utilisé des données satellites en Bavière du Nord entre 2017 et 2021 pour observer ce phénomène à grande échelle.

**Impact** – Ce retard réduit la survie des chenilles et diminue les dégâts sur les feuilles de plus de moitié. Cette méthode est plus efficace que les défenses chimiques de l’arbre, comme les tanins amers, et demande moins d’énergie. Cette découverte montre que les arbres ne réagissent pas seulement aux changements de température, mais aussi aux menaces des insectes, ce qui change notre compréhension du printemps en forêt.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent étudier ce mécanisme plus en détail et voir comment il aide les forêts à s’adapter au changement climatique. Cela pourrait améliorer la protection des forêts et les prévisions de leur croissance.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les chênes peuvent retarder la croissance des feuilles après une forte attaque de chenilles, utilisant un simple ajustement de timing pour se protéger et réduire les dégâts.
articles.personality_title_es: Los robles retrasan la primavera para dejar sin comida a las orugas hambrientas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En primavera, muchas orugas nacen justo cuando los robles crecen hojas nuevas. Estas hojas jóvenes son suaves y llenas de nutrientes, perfectas para que las orugas se alimenten.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos descubrieron que cuando los robles son atacados fuertemente por orugas un año, retrasan el crecimiento de sus hojas nuevas unos tres días la primavera siguiente. Este pequeño retraso hace que las orugas nazcan antes de que las hojas se abran, dejándolas sin comida. Los investigadores usaron datos satelitales del norte de Baviera entre 2017 y 2021 para ver este patrón en grandes bosques.

**Impacto** – Este retraso reduce la supervivencia de las orugas y disminuye el daño a las hojas en más de la mitad. Funciona mejor que las defensas químicas del árbol, como los taninos amargos, y usa menos energía. El descubrimiento muestra que los árboles no solo responden al clima, sino también a las amenazas de insectos, cambiando cómo entendemos la primavera en los bosques.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren estudiar esta defensa con más detalle y ver cómo ayuda a los bosques a adaptarse al cambio climático. Esto podría mejorar la protección forestal y los pronósticos del crecimiento de los bosques.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los robles pueden retrasar el crecimiento de hojas tras ataques fuertes de orugas, usando un truco de tiempo natural para protegerse y reducir daños.
articles.image_prompt: A serene forest scene at early spring, where a majestic oak tree with tightly closed leaf buds stands prominently, its branches gently holding back a swarm of delicate, translucent caterpillar silhouettes hovering nearby, all rendered in soft, earthy tones with warm golden light filtering through mist, symbolizing the tree’s subtle, natural defense delaying spring to starve the caterpillars.

articles.title: Guest Idea: How To Spread Awareness About Issues That Matter
articles.summary:
Trash can? Storage container? The dilemma of what should be done with all types of...
The post Guest Idea: How To Spread Awareness About Issues That Matter appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Trash can? Storage container? The dilemma of what should be done with all types of old batteries may seem trifling, but choosing incorrectly is detrimental to our planet and against the law in many states. As a junior in high school, I chose to help people make the right choice by starting an awareness campaign, the Battery Recycling Initiative.

The first step to starting an awareness campaign is identifying the issue you wish to advocate for. Through research and observation, I noted that many of us, including people in my own community, were unaware of the consequences of improper battery disposal on our environment. In fact, according to Recycling Today, 41% of Americans are unaware of the dangers of improper battery disposal.

The second step is to set the scope of your campaign. Are you planning on only advocating locally, globally, or a mix of both? Which specific areas should you advocate in to effectively spread awareness?

For my campaign, I chose to start locally and move globally. To find out if a local battery recycling campaign would be effective in my community, I decided to survey residents in Houston, TX and found out that more than 50% of the residents did not recycle batteries and about 14% only recycled certain types of batteries.

Step 1: Identify the issue and scope of your initiative

How does one start taking inititative? It is simple. Get people to listen. There were three strategies I used to increase awareness about battery recycling:

Provide information digitally and physically Engage people through interaction and face-to-face conversations Provide resources for people to take action.

These strategies tend to work for the majority of awareness campaigns: indirectly educate people (this could be through flyers, websites posts, etc.), directly educate people through in-person events, and give them a convenient method to take action. Why are these strategies effective?

Because through these 3 different ways to reach out to and engage people, you can cover most of the reasons why people may choose not to participate in resolving an issue. For example, the three main reasons why people don’t recycle batteries are:

people do not know they can recycle batteries.

recycling batteries is not convenient for some people.

they do not know where to recycle, or people do not have the will to recycle- they see recycling as insignificant, or they are ignorant of grave consequences for future generations.

All three of these problems can be combatted using the three strategies. Through indirect education, people learn that batteries can be recycled and where they can recycle them. Direct education empowers people to recycle, to take action, which combats the lack of will problem. Finally, providing resources to residents, in my case by placing battery recycling bins at my community clubhouses, combatted the lack of convenience aspect.

Step 2: Use the Three Strategies

Strategy 1 – Indirect Education

The first step to indirectly educating people is to ensure your information is accurate. I did plenty of research and talked to various battery recycling centers- like the Fort Bend County Battery Recycling Center- to ensure my information was accurate. The next step is choosing which methods of indirect education you wish to utilize. I chose to provide information via flyers, and use a QR code to help people locate their nearest battery recycling center, to give people quick and easy means to receive the information. I chose to utilize social media as my 2nd method to spread my initiative over a more globalized scope.

Strategy 2 – Direct Education

The main goal of direct education is to empower people to take action and to support/join your initiative. By interacting with people via face-to-face conversations, you retain the person’s attention a lot better than indirect means. By building a connection with the person you converse with, it encourages them to take part in the initiative.

For example, I participated in my community’s Green Day event where I set up a small booth and talked with residents about battery recycling. I remember having a conversation with this resident who was surprised to learn she could recycle batteries.

Many other residents told me they would just store old batteries in a container, not knowing what to do with them. One of my favorite interactions was with this lady who was so inspired by my initiative; she offered to help me out with anything I needed. While direct education does not reach that large of an audience, every meaningful connection you develop carries a depth of impact that numbers alone cannot measure- it has the potential to ripple out and influence countless others.

Strategy 3 – Providing a Convenient Method to Take Action

Convenience and availability play a big part in people’s will to take action. In fact, according to a study done by the Carton Council, these two factors contributed the most towards people’s will to recycle.

By appealing to people’s need for convenience, you spread awareness more effectively and grow your initiative by influencing people to act. I applied this idea by placing two battery recycling bins at both of my community clubhouses. I ended up receiving around 1,000 old batteries from those bins within two weeks, which I then safely recycled by taping the points of contact- this helps prevent fires due to batteries.

Have the Will and a Vision to Make an Impact

It may seem like you are just one person who cannot make an impact, but with a strong will and right vision you can achieve success. Your age, position, or location does not matter: I am just a Junior in high school living in a suburban area, but what does matter is you care and you have the heart to do something about it.

I urge you to utilize these methods and strategies to spread awareness about issues that matter to you, to make an impact. To quote the well-known, “Be the change you wish to see in the world.”

About the Author

Swara Bhatt is a high school junior who loves to paint, read, and watch movies in her free time. She hopes to make the world a better place, one step at a time. If you are interested in seeing updates about the battery recycling initiative, follow the project on Instagram: @batteryrecyclingintitative
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful grassroots environmental initiative led by a high school student to increase battery recycling awareness and action in her community, which has measurable positive environmental impact and potential for broader influence. It provides detailed strategies and results, showing substance and significance beyond a small niche.
articles.personality_title: High school student launches local battery recycling campaign with real results
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people do not know how to properly dispose of old batteries, which can harm the environment and is illegal in some places. A high school junior noticed this problem in her community and wanted to help.

**What happened** – Swara Bhatt, a junior from Houston, Texas, started the Battery Recycling Initiative to teach people about safe battery disposal. She used three main strategies: sharing information through flyers and social media, talking directly to people at local events, and making recycling easier by placing battery bins in community centers. In just two weeks, the bins collected around 1,000 batteries.

**Impact** – This campaign shows how one person can make a difference by combining education and convenience. It helped many residents learn about battery recycling and made it simpler for them to act. The project also proved that face-to-face talks encourage people to participate more than just reading information.

**What's next step** – Swara plans to expand the campaign beyond her local area by using social media to reach more people. She encourages others to use similar methods to raise awareness about issues they care about.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A high school student’s clear plan and effort helped her community recycle batteries safely and inspired others to take action.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une lycéenne lance une campagne locale de recyclage des batteries avec des résultats concrets
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de gens ne savent pas comment jeter correctement leurs vieilles piles, ce qui peut nuire à l’environnement et est interdit dans certains endroits. Une élève de lycée a remarqué ce problème dans sa communauté et a voulu agir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Swara Bhatt, une élève de Houston, Texas, a créé l’initiative de recyclage des batteries pour informer les gens sur leur élimination sûre. Elle a utilisé trois stratégies principales : partager des informations via des flyers et les réseaux sociaux, parler directement aux habitants lors d’événements locaux, et faciliter le recyclage en installant des bacs dans des centres communautaires. En deux semaines, ces bacs ont recueilli environ 1 000 piles.

**Impact** – Cette campagne montre comment une seule personne peut faire la différence en combinant éducation et facilité d’action. Elle a aidé de nombreux habitants à mieux comprendre le recyclage des piles et à agir plus facilement. Le projet a aussi prouvé que les échanges en personne motivent plus que la simple lecture d’informations.

**Prochaine étape** – Swara prévoit d’étendre la campagne au-delà de sa région locale grâce aux réseaux sociaux. Elle encourage les autres à utiliser des méthodes similaires pour sensibiliser sur des sujets qui leur tiennent à cœur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’effort clair d’une lycéenne a aidé sa communauté à recycler les piles en toute sécurité et a inspiré d’autres à agir.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudiante de secundaria lanza campaña local de reciclaje de baterías con resultados reales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas no saben cómo desechar correctamente las baterías viejas, lo que puede dañar el medio ambiente y es ilegal en algunos lugares. Una estudiante de secundaria notó este problema en su comunidad y quiso ayudar.

**Qué pasó** – Swara Bhatt, una estudiante de Houston, Texas, inició la Iniciativa de Reciclaje de Baterías para enseñar a las personas sobre la eliminación segura de baterías. Usó tres estrategias principales: compartir información mediante volantes y redes sociales, hablar directamente con la gente en eventos locales, y facilitar el reciclaje colocando contenedores en centros comunitarios. En solo dos semanas, los contenedores reunieron alrededor de 1,000 baterías.

**Impacto** – Esta campaña demuestra cómo una persona puede hacer una diferencia combinando educación y conveniencia. Ayudó a muchas personas a aprender sobre el reciclaje de baterías y facilitó que actuaran. El proyecto también mostró que las conversaciones cara a cara motivan más que solo leer información.

**Próximo paso** – Swara planea expandir la campaña fuera de su área local usando las redes sociales para llegar a más personas. Anima a otros a usar métodos similares para crear conciencia sobre los temas que les importan.

**Resumen en una frase** – El plan claro y el esfuerzo de una estudiante ayudaron a su comunidad a reciclar baterías de forma segura e inspiraron a otros a actuar.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing battery-shaped tree with branches made of colorful recycled batteries, roots extending into the earth symbolizing community support, surrounded by abstract silhouettes of people engaging in conversation and placing batteries into nearby recycling bins, all rendered in soft, natural tones.

articles.title: American homes need heat pumps, not space heaters
articles.content:
If you want to ditch your gas furnace and heat your home more cleanly and efficiently, you need to scale up one of your kitchen appliances. The first option is “electric resistance heating,” better known as a space heater, which acts like a giant toaster to warm a room instead of bread. The second is a heat pump, which extracts warmth from even freezing outdoor air and pumps it indoors, like a refrigerator moves heat from inside the box to the kitchen. (That’s why the back of your fridge feels warm, by the way.)

Energy experts say that to bring down greenhouse gas emissions and improve human health, we need to replace toxic gas furnaces and boilers with heat pumps ASAP. Less talked about, though, is that we also need to replace those giant toasters with giant reverse refrigerators, which would make homes more comfortable and more efficient, and therefore cheaper to heat.

According to a new report from the nonprofit energy group RMI, one in five homes in the United States is heated primarily with electric resistance heating. Replacing those devices with heat pumps would save households an average of $1,530 a year, or $20 billion annually across the country. (The calculations included only single-family homes, not multifamily units like apartment buildings.) At the same time, demand on the electrical grid would fall significantly, while total carbon emissions from homes making the switch to heat pumps for climate control and water heating would plummet by about 40 percent. “There’s a lot of benefits to the grid, which translate to lower rates as well,” said Ryan Shea, a manager in RMI’s carbon-free buildings program. “Then, of course, there’s using less energy.”

Electric heat pumps work their magic with a trick of physics: By changing the pressure of refrigerants, they draw warmth from outdoor air or liquids coursing underground, then bring it indoors. (In the summer, the process reverses, cooling an indoor space like a traditional air conditioning unit.) They’re ultraefficient because unlike a furnace or space heater — which generate warmth by burning fossil fuels or using electricity — these appliances simply transfer heat from one place to another. Accordingly, heat pumps have a “coefficient of performance,” or COP, of around three, meaning they produce three units of heat for every unit of electricity used. In other words, they’re 300 percent efficient. That’s three times as efficient as electric resistance heating, which has a COP of one, while even the most efficient gas furnaces operate well below that.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

In all kinds of homes, heat pumps are replacing electric resistance heating or gas furnaces. If you don’t have ducting, heat pumps come in units that embed in walls to exchange between outdoor and indoor air. If you have ducting, an indoor unit replaces the furnace and connects to an outdoor one, which exchanges the heat. If you also have an AC unit that has reached the end of its life, subbing in a heat pump will give you both cooling and ultraefficient heating. “That’s kind of the right trigger point for a lot of people to start thinking about heat pumps,” Shea said, “is when their air conditioner needs replacing.”

The next generation of heat pump is targeting apartment-dwellers, too. A company called Gradient, for instance, has been working with building owners and public housing authorities to deploy its units, which slip over window sills like saddles and plug into a standard wall outlet. The appliance can swap in for old window AC units, giving tenants clean heating, not just cooling.

The idea is to quickly and cheaply deploy these appliances in large buildings, without landlords having to retrofit each unit if they, say, get rid of the structure’s central fossil-fuel boiler. Gradient says that in less than two weeks, it installed 277 of them in a Providence, Rhode Island public housing development that previously used electric resistance heating. “It is very straightforward and a huge energy win for them,” said Vince Romanin, the company’s founder and chief technology officer. “You’re not just saving money. You are providing a dramatically better service, because you’re adding cooling.”

Still, the RMI report notes, the U.S. builds nearly 1.5 million homes each year, 200,000 of them with electric resistance heating. It also installs a million AC units annually in homes with electric resistance heating, when those could instead be heat pumps that’d save occupants money. The trick, then, is for policymakers and utilities to incentivize these efficient appliances with rebates and the like. That’s what helped Maine reach its goal of installing 100,000 heat pumps two years ahead of schedule — by next year, it hopes to install 175,000 more.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The U.S., though, can’t simply replace all of its furnaces and space heaters with heat pumps and call it a day, energy experts said. It must happen alongside a push to make homes more efficient, like by installing proper insulation and double-pane windows. That is, a home needs to retain more heat in the winter and cool air in the summer, so a heat pump would need to run less. “Step one, don’t burn fossil fuels in your home, basically,” said Gernot Wagner, a climate economist at Columbia Business School, who wasn’t involved in the new report. “Step two: insulate, insulate, insulate. And both of those go hand-in-hand.”

The grid, too, needs upgrades if heat pumps are to reach their full potential. For one, ideally you’re powering them with electricity coming entirely from renewables like wind and solar, otherwise you’re still burning fossil fuels to warm homes. (Though to be clear, because heat pumps are so efficient, this is still better than sticking with gas furnaces.) And two, heat pumps join electric vehicles and induction stoves in increasing demand on the grid. Utilities are already making upgrades to handle all this electrification, like installing huge battery banks to store renewable energy to use when the sun doesn’t shine and wind doesn’t blow. They’re also experimenting with vehicle-to-grid technology, or V2G, which allows EVs to send power to the grid when demand is highest.

If the U.S. is really going to wean itself off fossil fuels, it needs all these systems to work in concert: More renewables, more batteries, fewer giant toasters.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses the significant environmental and economic benefits of replacing inefficient electric resistance heating and gas furnaces with highly efficient heat pumps in American homes. This transition can reduce carbon emissions by about 40%, lower household energy costs substantially, and ease demand on the electrical grid, contributing to climate action and improved public health. The story provides detailed context on technology, scale, and policy efforts, highlighting a meaningful, broad impact on society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: Heat pumps offer cleaner, cheaper home heating than space heaters in the U.S.
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many American homes use gas furnaces or electric space heaters to stay warm. These methods waste energy and add pollution. Heat pumps are a newer technology that can heat homes more efficiently by moving heat from outside to inside, even when it’s cold.

**What happened** – A report from the nonprofit group RMI shows that one in five U.S. homes uses electric space heaters, also called electric resistance heating. Replacing these with heat pumps could save a typical household about $1,530 each year. Heat pumps work like refrigerators but in reverse, transferring heat without burning fuel. They are about three times more efficient than space heaters or gas furnaces.

**Impact** – Switching to heat pumps could reduce carbon emissions from homes by 40% and lower the energy demand on the electric grid. This means cleaner air and cheaper electricity bills. Heat pumps also provide both heating and cooling, making homes more comfortable year-round. New designs can fit apartments easily, helping more people benefit.

**What's next step** – Policymakers and utility companies need to encourage heat pump use through rebates and programs. States like Maine have already made progress by installing many heat pumps early. Homes also need better insulation so heat pumps don’t have to work as hard. The electric grid must improve to handle more clean energy and demand.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using heat pumps instead of space heaters or gas furnaces can save money, reduce pollution, and make homes more comfortable across the U.S.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les pompes à chaleur offrent un chauffage domestique plus propre et moins cher que les radiateurs d’appoint aux États-Unis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses maisons américaines utilisent des chaudières à gaz ou des radiateurs électriques pour se chauffer. Ces méthodes gaspillent de l’énergie et polluent. Les pompes à chaleur sont une technologie plus récente qui chauffe les maisons plus efficacement en transférant la chaleur de l’extérieur vers l’intérieur, même quand il fait froid.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un rapport de l’organisation à but non lucratif RMI montre qu’une maison sur cinq aux États-Unis utilise des radiateurs électriques, aussi appelés chauffage par résistance électrique. Les remplacer par des pompes à chaleur pourrait faire économiser environ 1 530 dollars par an à un foyer moyen. Les pompes à chaleur fonctionnent comme un réfrigérateur inversé, transférant la chaleur sans brûler de combustible. Elles sont environ trois fois plus efficaces que les radiateurs ou chaudières à gaz.

**Impact** – Passer aux pompes à chaleur pourrait réduire de 40 % les émissions de carbone des maisons et diminuer la demande d’énergie sur le réseau électrique. Cela signifie un air plus propre et des factures d’électricité moins élevées. Les pompes à chaleur chauffent et rafraîchissent, rendant les maisons plus confortables toute l’année. De nouveaux modèles s’adaptent facilement aux appartements, étendant leurs avantages.

**Prochaine étape** – Les décideurs et les compagnies d’électricité doivent encourager l’usage des pompes à chaleur avec des aides financières et des programmes. Des États comme le Maine ont déjà avancé en installant beaucoup de pompes à chaleur rapidement. Les maisons doivent aussi être mieux isolées pour que les pompes travaillent moins. Le réseau électrique doit être amélioré pour gérer plus d’énergie propre et de demande.

**Une phrase clé** – Utiliser des pompes à chaleur au lieu de radiateurs électriques ou chaudières à gaz permet d’économiser de l’argent, de réduire la pollution et d’améliorer le confort des maisons aux États-Unis.
articles.personality_title_es: Las bombas de calor ofrecen calefacción doméstica más limpia y barata que los calefactores eléctricos en EE. UU.
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas casas en Estados Unidos usan calentadores de gas o eléctricos para mantenerse calientes. Estos métodos desperdician energía y contaminan. Las bombas de calor son una tecnología más nueva que calienta las casas de forma más eficiente al mover el calor del exterior hacia el interior, incluso cuando hace frío.

**Qué pasó** – Un informe del grupo sin fines de lucro RMI muestra que una de cada cinco casas en EE. UU. usa calentadores eléctricos, llamados calefacción por resistencia eléctrica. Reemplazarlos con bombas de calor podría ahorrar a una familia típica unos 1,530 dólares al año. Las bombas de calor funcionan como refrigeradores al revés, transfiriendo calor sin quemar combustible. Son unas tres veces más eficientes que los calentadores eléctricos o las calderas de gas.

**Impacto** – Cambiar a bombas de calor podría reducir las emisiones de carbono de las casas en un 40 % y bajar la demanda de energía en la red eléctrica. Esto significa aire más limpio y facturas de electricidad más bajas. Las bombas de calor también ofrecen calefacción y enfriamiento, haciendo las casas más cómodas todo el año. Nuevos diseños pueden instalarse fácilmente en apartamentos, beneficiando a más personas.

**Próximo paso** – Los gobiernos y compañías eléctricas deben fomentar el uso de bombas de calor con incentivos y programas. Estados como Maine ya avanzaron instalando muchas bombas de calor antes de lo previsto. Las casas también necesitan mejor aislamiento para que las bombas trabajen menos. La red eléctrica debe mejorar para manejar más energía limpia y demanda.

**Una frase clave** – Usar bombas de calor en lugar de calentadores eléctricos o calderas de gas puede ahorrar dinero, reducir la contaminación y mejorar el confort en las casas de EE. UU.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy home warmly lit from within, with a stylized heat pump unit gently drawing glowing, soft golden warmth from a cool blue outdoor environment, contrasted against a dull, oversized electric space heater resembling a muted toaster fading into the background; the scene uses simple, natural earth tones and soft blues to symbolize clean, efficient heating replacing old, wasteful methods.

articles.title: To move beyond GDP, don’t ignore environmental economists
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 05 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01299-wSustainable development will only be achieved when governments base decisions on human skills and natural resources, not just gross domestic product.
articles.content:
‘Beyond GDP’ approaches must take natural capital and planetary health into account.Credit: Matjaz Corel/Alamy

The world seems ready to move beyond gross domestic product (GDP), a measure of economic growth, and towards metrics that are more representative of sustainability and people’s well-being. United Nation member states ratified this move in 2024, and the World Bank concurs.

A UN group, tasked last year with recommending how this transition should work, released a draft of its interim report in November last year. A final report is expected on 7 May. The proposed framework has drawn strong reactions from many experts in beyond-GDP metrics, few of whom were part of the group. In short, the report is vastly complicated and untethered from the substantial body of work that has been gathered over many decades in this field.

How to measure a good life — tips for moving beyond GDP

The framework for the transition aims to cover all bases of well-being — including health, education and ‘subjective well-being’. It rests on three foundations (peace, respect for the planet and human rights). But it fails to recognize the core dependence of human needs on nature. And because it isn’t firmly grounded in economic and ecological sciences, the proposal lacks robustness and credibility.

Without solid backing from environmental economists, the UN will struggle to provide an authoritative pathway to move governments beyond GDP.

I have worked on such alternative measures for three decades. I agree that GDP, which focuses on markets, provides a useful statement on the level of economic activities in a nation. But it is unable to factor in contributions from nature, including the value of a stable climate or accessibility of clean air and water. GDP omits aspects of human capital, such as people’s health, skills, knowledge and cultural practices. Therefore, decisions based solely on GDP can be misguided because they consider only part of the picture.

Heatwaves, droughts and floods, land degradation, species extinction and pollution all undermine human well-being. Any measure of sustainability must assess and account for changes in natural resources. Destroying them to manufacture goods and produce services will hamper, not boost, growth and prosperity.

Beyond growth — why we need to agree on an alternative to GDP now

Instead, I and many environmental economists would like to see governments assess ‘inclusive wealth’. This includes human and natural capital as well as the manufactured capital that falls under GDP. It includes infrastructure, the ability of a country’s population to be productive, and both renewable and non-renewable natural assets. Assigning monetary value to these three types of capital (human, natural and manufactured) provides an estimate of inclusive wealth, which is more representative of the health of a society than is income alone. Economists know how to calculate inclusive wealth. But it remains to be integrated into the UN System of National Accounts, which is used by most governments. Several countries, including the United Kingdom, Canada and Pakistan, have already started calculating their wealth including natural capital.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant and positive shift in how governments measure progress by moving beyond GDP to include natural capital and sustainability, which has broad implications for environmental policy and human well-being worldwide. It highlights the importance of integrating environmental economics into global decision-making frameworks, a meaningful advancement for sustainable development.
articles.personality_title: New ways to measure progress aim to include nature and people’s well-being
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, countries have used a number called gross domestic product (GDP) to measure how well their economies are doing. GDP counts the value of goods and services made but does not include important things like clean air, healthy ecosystems, or people’s health and skills.

**What happened** – In 2024, United Nations member countries agreed to move beyond GDP and find better ways to measure progress that include nature and human well-being. A UN group has been working on a plan and will release a final report soon. However, some experts say the current plan misses important ideas from environmental economists who study how natural resources and human skills add value.

**Impact** – This shift is important because it recognizes that using GDP alone can give a wrong picture of how healthy and wealthy a country really is. For example, cutting down forests to produce goods might increase GDP but harm the environment and people’s quality of life. Including “inclusive wealth,” which adds natural resources and human capital to the mix, gives a fuller and more accurate view of a country’s true wealth.

**What’s next step** – Governments and international groups will need to work closely with environmental economists to improve the new measurement system. Some countries like the UK, Canada, and Pakistan have already started measuring their wealth this way. The challenge is to make these ideas part of official statistics used worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Moving beyond GDP to include nature and human skills will help countries make better decisions for a healthier planet and society.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles façons de mesurer le progrès intègrent la nature et le bien-être des personnes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les pays utilisent un chiffre appelé produit intérieur brut (PIB) pour mesurer la santé de leur économie. Le PIB compte la valeur des biens et services produits, mais n’inclut pas des éléments importants comme l’air pur, les écosystèmes sains ou la santé et les compétences des personnes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, les pays membres des Nations Unies ont décidé d’aller au-delà du PIB et de trouver de meilleures façons de mesurer le progrès en incluant la nature et le bien-être humain. Un groupe de l’ONU travaille sur un plan qui sera publié bientôt. Cependant, certains experts disent que ce plan oublie des idées importantes des économistes de l’environnement qui étudient comment les ressources naturelles et les compétences humaines ajoutent de la valeur.

**Impact** – Ce changement est important car il reconnaît que le PIB seul peut donner une fausse image de la richesse réelle d’un pays. Par exemple, couper des forêts pour produire des biens peut augmenter le PIB mais nuire à l’environnement et à la qualité de vie. Inclure la « richesse inclusive », qui ajoute les ressources naturelles et le capital humain, donne une image plus complète et plus juste de la richesse d’un pays.

**Prochaine étape** – Les gouvernements et les groupes internationaux doivent travailler avec les économistes de l’environnement pour améliorer ce nouveau système de mesure. Certains pays comme le Royaume-Uni, le Canada et le Pakistan ont déjà commencé à mesurer leur richesse de cette manière. Le défi est d’intégrer ces idées dans les statistiques officielles utilisées partout.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Aller au-delà du PIB pour inclure la nature et les compétences humaines aidera les pays à prendre de meilleures décisions pour une planète et une société plus saines.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas formas de medir el progreso incluyen la naturaleza y el bienestar de las personas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los países han usado un número llamado producto interno bruto (PIB) para medir cómo va su economía. El PIB cuenta el valor de los bienes y servicios producidos, pero no incluye cosas importantes como el aire limpio, los ecosistemas saludables o la salud y habilidades de las personas.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, los países miembros de las Naciones Unidas acordaron ir más allá del PIB y buscar mejores formas de medir el progreso que incluyan la naturaleza y el bienestar humano. Un grupo de la ONU está trabajando en un plan que se publicará pronto. Sin embargo, algunos expertos dicen que el plan actual no incluye ideas importantes de economistas ambientales que estudian cómo los recursos naturales y las habilidades humanas agregan valor.

**Impacto** – Este cambio es importante porque reconoce que usar solo el PIB puede dar una imagen equivocada de qué tan saludable y rico es un país realmente. Por ejemplo, talar bosques para producir bienes puede aumentar el PIB pero dañar el medio ambiente y la calidad de vida. Incluir la “riqueza inclusiva”, que suma recursos naturales y capital humano, ofrece una visión más completa y precisa de la verdadera riqueza de un país.

**Próximo paso** – Los gobiernos y organizaciones internacionales deberán trabajar con economistas ambientales para mejorar este nuevo sistema de medición. Algunos países como Reino Unido, Canadá y Pakistán ya comenzaron a medir su riqueza de esta manera. El reto es hacer que estas ideas formen parte de las estadísticas oficiales usadas en todo el mundo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Ir más allá del PIB para incluir la naturaleza y las habilidades humanas ayudará a los países a tomar mejores decisiones para un planeta y sociedad más saludables.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a balanced scale where one side holds a globe entwined with lush ecosystems and flowing rivers representing natural capital, while the other side holds stylized icons of human skills and infrastructure symbolizing human and manufactured capital, all set against a gentle, earthy background that conveys harmony and interconnectedness.
